tipsAndTricks: .codex/instructions.md is your persistent context file — write it once and Codex reads it every run
codex exec for one-shot non-interactive runs — great for CI and automation
--approval-mode auto for trusted repos; --approval-mode suggest for exploratory work
Codex reads full git history as context — commit your work before starting big tasks
Feed it the whole repo not just one file — Codex handles large context well
--quiet suppresses thinking output for cleaner script integration
Sub-agent support is built in — Codex spawns parallel workers for large tasks automatically

quickStart.title: Get productive in 30 seconds
steps: Inside your repo, run `codex --full-auto "fix the failing test, run the smallest relevant test, and show the diff"` for a fast one-shot edit loop.
For interactive work, run `codex` then use `/plan` before big changes and `/review` before you commit.
If Codex needs more scope, add writable roots with `--add-dir` instead of jumping straight to full access.
Use `codex resume --last` to continue the same thread tomorrow instead of re-explaining context.
Keep a repo-level `AGENTS.md` with build/test commands and constraints so Codex stops making the same mistakes.

dailyWorkflows.title: Quick one-shot fix
dailyWorkflows.description: Best default for small bugs, lint fixes, test failures, or targeted refactors. Ask for the smallest fix plus validation so you get code and proof, not just code.
dailyWorkflows.command: codex exec --full-auto "fix the failing test, run the narrowest relevant test, and summarize what changed"

dailyWorkflows.title: Interactive debugging in a real repo
dailyWorkflows.description: Use the TUI when you want to steer, inspect diffs, answer questions, and approve actions live. Better than `exec` when the problem is ambiguous.
dailyWorkflows.command: codex "investigate why login sometimes fails after refresh; explain root cause before editing"

dailyWorkflows.title: Plan first, then implement
dailyWorkflows.description: For risky or multi-file changes, force a planning pass first. This reduces thrash and catches missing constraints early.
dailyWorkflows.command: codex "propose a step-by-step plan to add rate limiting to the API; do not edit yet"

dailyWorkflows.title: Resume yesterday's thread
dailyWorkflows.description: Huge time saver. Reuse the transcript, approvals, and plan history instead of pasting context back in.

dailyWorkflows.title: Continue a past non-interactive run
dailyWorkflows.description: Great when an earlier `exec` run found issues but did not finish the implementation. Lets Codex build on prior findings.
dailyWorkflows.command: codex exec resume --last "implement the fix you proposed and run focused validation"

dailyWorkflows.title: Review your local diff before commit
dailyWorkflows.description: Use Codex as a second reviewer after you or Codex changed code. Good for catching missing tests, regressions, and risky assumptions.
dailyWorkflows.command: codex exec review "review the current changes for correctness, regressions, and missing tests"

dailyWorkflows.title: Patch across multiple directories safely
dailyWorkflows.description: When frontend and backend live side-by-side, add extra writable roots explicitly. This is the power-user alternative to broad full access.
dailyWorkflows.command: codex --cd apps/frontend --add-dir ../backend --add-dir ../shared "implement the API + UI change end to end"

dailyWorkflows.title: Headless/remote login setup
dailyWorkflows.description: When browser auth breaks in SSH, WSL, containers, or headless boxes, use device auth instead of fighting localhost callback issues.

dailyWorkflows.title: Machine-readable CI run
dailyWorkflows.description: Use JSONL output when another script needs progress events and the final answer. Pair it with a file for the last assistant message.
dailyWorkflows.command: codex exec --json -o /tmp/codex-last.txt "summarize the CI failure and propose the smallest fix"

dailyWorkflows.title: Launch a Codex Cloud task from the terminal
dailyWorkflows.description: Triage, start, and apply Codex Cloud tasks without leaving the terminal. Use --attempts for best-of-N runs on important tasks.
dailyWorkflows.command:
codex cloud exec --env ENV_ID "implement the feature described in the issue"
codex cloud exec --env ENV_ID --attempts 3 "fix the bug with three candidate solutions"

powerMoves.title: Parallel worktree army
powerMoves.description: Create several git worktrees and run one `codex exec --full-auto` task in each. Use this for parallel bugfixes, audits, or independent feature slices.
powerMoves.command: git worktree add ../repo-bug-1 -b bug-1 && git worktree add ../repo-bug-2 -b bug-2 && (cd ../repo-bug-1 && codex exec --full-auto "fix bug A and run focused tests") & (cd ../repo-bug-2 && codex exec --full-auto "fix bug B and run focused tests") & wait
powerMoves.why: Beginners run one long thread. Power users isolate context and file changes per task, which reduces agent confusion, merge pain, and context bloat.

powerMoves.title: Plan → implement → review loop
powerMoves.description: Use one thread for planning, then implementation, then a dedicated review pass with explicit review criteria.
powerMoves.command: codex "plan the migration"  # then /review after changes
powerMoves.why: This gets better results than treating Codex like a one-shot code generator. You turn it into planner, implementer, and reviewer in sequence.

powerMoves.title: Scoped autonomy instead of YOLO
powerMoves.description: Keep Codex in workspace-write and widen scope only with `--add-dir`, repo config, or network toggles when needed.
powerMoves.command: codex --cd services/api --add-dir ../shared "update the contract and consumers"
powerMoves.why: Power users rarely jump straight to `--yolo`. They give exactly enough access for the task, which is safer and usually easier to reason about.

powerMoves.title: Codify your workflow in AGENTS.md
powerMoves.description: Put build/test/lint commands, architecture constraints, and 'done when' rules in AGENTS.md so Codex loads them automatically.
powerMoves.command: codex  # in a repo with AGENTS.md describing conventions and validation commands
powerMoves.why: This removes repeated prompt boilerplate and makes Codex more consistent across sessions and teammates.

powerMoves.title: Use `/compact` before the thread gets dumb
powerMoves.description: After a long debugging or implementation session, compact the transcript to preserve key facts while freeing context.
powerMoves.why: Long unmaintained threads degrade. Compacting keeps the important state while reducing context rot.

powerMoves.title: JSON schema output for structured pipelines
powerMoves.description: When you need Codex to feed another tool, constrain the final answer with a schema instead of regexing prose.
powerMoves.command: codex exec --output-schema ./result.schema.json "analyze the repo and return structured risks"
powerMoves.why: This is much more robust for CI, dashboards, checklists, and bulk analysis than parsing human-oriented text.

powerMoves.title: Multi-agent for bounded parallel work
powerMoves.description: Subagents are enabled by default. Ask Codex to spawn one agent per concern (e.g. explorer, reviewer, worker) and wait for consolidated results. Define custom agents as TOML files in `.codex/agents/` (project) or `~/.codex/agents/` (global).
powerMoves.command:
codex  # ask: 'Spawn one agent per review point, wait for all, summarize'
# Custom agent: .codex/agents/reviewer.toml with name/description/developer_instructions
powerMoves.why: Useful when a task is naturally parallel. Power users use it for exploration, review, and evidence gathering—not for every tiny change. Each subagent consumes its own tokens, so use intentionally.

powerMoves.title: Device auth + copied auth cache for remote boxes
powerMoves.description: Authenticate once on a local machine, then reuse trusted auth on headless machines when browser callbacks are painful.
powerMoves.why: This avoids one of the most common setup blockers for SSH boxes, containers, and remote dev environments.

gotchas.problem: Codex exec refuses to run outside a Git repo by default.
gotchas.solution: Run it inside a repo, `git init` first, or deliberately bypass with `codex exec --skip-git-repo-check ...` if you know what you're doing.
It means workspace-write plus on-request approvals. Codex will still stop for out-of-workspace edits or network access.

gotchas.problem: Protected paths like `.git` and `.codex` can still be read-only even in writable mode.
gotchas.solution: Expect commands like `git commit` or direct edits under protected paths to require approval or fail under the sandbox.

gotchas.problem: Browser login fails on SSH, WSL, remote boxes, or locked-down networking.
gotchas.solution: Use `codex login --device-auth`, or authenticate locally and copy `~/.codex/auth.json` only within trusted environments.

Prefer workspace-write plus `--add-dir` and selective config changes. Use dangerous full access only inside an externally sandboxed environment you trust. Note: `--yolo` also defaults web search to live results instead of cached.

gotchas.problem: Long threads get worse over time.
gotchas.solution: Use one thread per task, `codex resume --last` for continuation, `/fork` when the task branches, and `/compact` when context gets bloated.

gotchas.problem: Web search behavior changes with sandbox/access level.
gotchas.solution: Default local mode uses cached web search. `--search` forces live results, and full-access/YOLO modes default to live web results. Treat all web content as untrusted.

gotchas.problem: People forget to tell Codex how to validate the change.
gotchas.solution: Always include 'run the narrowest relevant test/lint/typecheck' or put those commands in AGENTS.md so Codex can prove the change worked.

gotchas.problem: Running Codex in the wrong directory causes bad edits or irrelevant reasoning.
gotchas.solution: Use `--cd` explicitly and check `/status` before asking for multi-file work.

gotchas.problem: Subagent approval prompts from inactive threads can interrupt your main thread.
gotchas.solution: When an approval overlay appears showing a thread label, press `o` to open that thread and review it before approving or rejecting. In non-interactive/exec flows, actions needing new approval fail and surface the error back to the parent.

gotchas.problem: Shell completions can fail in zsh with `compdef` errors.
gotchas.solution: Add `autoload -Uz compinit && compinit` before `eval "$(codex completion zsh)"` in `~/.zshrc`.

cliFlags.description: Convenience alias for `--sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval on-request`. Best default for hands-off local edits inside the repo.
cliFlags.example: codex exec --full-auto "fix the flaky test and run it 5 times"
cliFlags.useCase: Daily one-shot fixes, focused refactors, and scripted local automation with sane safety rails.

cliFlags.description: Disables both sandboxing and approval prompts. Highest-risk mode.
cliFlags.useCase: Only inside a disposable VM/container or another environment that is already strongly sandboxed.

cliFlags.example: codex --sandbox read-only "explain this service and identify likely bugs"
cliFlags.useCase: When you want to reason safely before allowing edits, or deliberately widen/narrow execution scope.

cliFlags.description: Controls when Codex asks before executing commands: `untrusted`, `on-request`, or `never` (plus deprecated `on-failure`).
cliFlags.useCase: Useful for CI, read-only audits, or tightening control during risky investigations.

cliFlags.description: Sets the working root without manually changing directories.
cliFlags.useCase: Great for monorepos, scripts, and launching Codex from anywhere.

cliFlags.description: Adds extra writable roots alongside the primary workspace.
cliFlags.example: codex --cd apps/web --add-dir ../packages/ui "update the component and its consumers"
cliFlags.useCase: The safe way to work across multiple packages or sibling repos.

cliFlags.description: Allows `codex exec` to run outside a Git repository.
cliFlags.example: codex exec --skip-git-repo-check "generate a tiny prototype in this temp dir"
cliFlags.useCase: Useful for scratch dirs, temp repros, and generated experiments.

cliFlags.description: Streams newline-delimited JSON events instead of human-formatted output in `exec` mode.
cliFlags.useCase: Automation, dashboards, CI pipelines, and any system that needs machine-readable progress.

cliFlags.description: Writes the final assistant message to a file in `exec` mode.
cliFlags.useCase: Handy when you want JSONL event logs plus one clean summary artifact.

cliFlags.description: Constrains the final response shape using a JSON Schema file.
cliFlags.example: codex exec --output-schema ./report.schema.json "audit dependencies and return structured findings"
cliFlags.useCase: Reliable downstream automation, reports, and structured audits.

cliFlags.description: Runs without persisting session files to disk.
cliFlags.example: codex exec --ephemeral "inspect this repo and summarize the architecture"
cliFlags.useCase: Temporary runs, sensitive environments, and cleaner automation hosts.

cliFlags.description: Selects the model for the run or session.
cliFlags.useCase: Use stronger reasoning for hard debugging/review or cheaper/faster models for light exploration.

cliFlags.description: Loads a named config profile from `config.toml`.
cliFlags.example: codex --profile deep-review "review the branch vs main"
cliFlags.useCase: Switching between personal modes like deep review, lightweight edit, or read-only audit.

cliFlags.description: Enables live web search instead of cached web results.
cliFlags.example: codex --search "check the latest Next.js docs for this API"
cliFlags.useCase: When you need fresh docs, release notes, or current ecosystem information.

cliFlags.description: Attaches one or more images to the initial prompt.
cliFlags.example: codex -i screenshot.png "explain this runtime error screen"
cliFlags.useCase: Debugging screenshots, design handoff, diagrams, and visual bug reports.

cliFlags.description: One-off override for any config key; values are parsed as TOML.
cliFlags.useCase: Advanced one-run tweaks without editing your persistent config.

slashCommands.description: Switches into planning mode before coding.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Before multi-step, risky, or ambiguous work.

slashCommands.description: Runs a dedicated local code review on your branch, commit, or working tree.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Before commit/push or after Codex made substantial edits.
When you want to tighten control or let Codex move faster without restarting.

slashCommands.description: Shows model, permissions, writable roots, and token/context state.
slashCommands.whenToUse: Any time results seem weird or you want to confirm Codex's environment.

slashCommands.description: Shows the Git diff including untracked files.
Summarizes earlier conversation to free context.
slashCommands.whenToUse: After long sessions to avoid context bloat.

slashCommands.description: Branches the current conversation into a new thread.
slashCommands.whenToUse: When exploring an alternative implementation or debugging path.

slashCommands.description: Loads a saved conversation from the session list.
slashCommands.whenToUse: To continue an older task without restating context.

slashCommands.description: Switches between active agent threads in multi-agent mode. Approval requests from inactive threads surface as an overlay showing the source thread label.
slashCommands.whenToUse: When parallel agents are running and you need to inspect, steer, or approve from a specific thread.

slashCommands.description: Configures which metadata appears in the TUI footer interactively.
slashCommands.whenToUse: When you want to customize what status info you see in the footer without editing config.

slashCommands.description: Scaffolds an `AGENTS.md` file in the current directory.
slashCommands.whenToUse: When setting up a repo so Codex learns your conventions once instead of every run.

slashCommands.description: Changes model (and reasoning when supported) mid-session.
slashCommands.whenToUse: When a task shifts from quick edits to deep debugging or vice versa.

slashCommands.description: Lists configured MCP tools available in this session.
slashCommands.whenToUse: When you expect external tools/docs/connectors and want to verify access.

config.whyItMatters: Set a strong default once so every session starts on the right model.
Defines how interrupt-y or autonomous Codex feels.
The core safety/productivity dial for local execution.
Needed for package installs, live docs fetches, and networked verification from workspace-write mode.
Choose between safer cached search, fresher live search, or no web search at all.
Useful for harder debugging, planning, and review tasks.
Controls concurrent subagent thread cap (default 6) and nesting depth (default 1). Increase max_depth only if you need recursive delegation—deeper nesting compounds token usage and latency.

config.example:
codex features list
codex features enable unified_exec
codex features disable shell_snapshot
config.whyItMatters: Inspect and toggle experimental feature flags. Writes to ~/.codex/config.toml (or profile if --profile is active). Replaces manual [features] edits for flags.
Can speed up repeated command execution in your shell environment.
Lets power users switch between repeatable modes like deep review, safe read-only, or quick edit.
Avoids plaintext token storage when the OS credential store is available.
Prevents accidental secret leakage to subprocesses while keeping needed env vars.
Power-user alerting hook for desktop notifications, webhooks, or CI updates when a turn completes.
Useful in sensitive environments where you do not want local transcript history saved.

proTips: The highest-leverage prompt pattern is: goal + relevant context/files + constraints + done-when. This consistently beats vague requests.
Always tell Codex how to verify success: specific test, lint, typecheck, or repro command. Otherwise it may stop at 'I changed the code.'
Use one thread per task. Resume the thread if the task continues; fork only if the task truly branches.
For big repos, mention files or set `--cd` explicitly so Codex starts in the right part of the tree.
Use `/review` even on your own changes. Codex is much more useful as a second reviewer than as a blind first drafter.
If a workflow repeats, move it into `AGENTS.md`, config, or a custom slash command/skill instead of rewriting the same mega-prompt.
In monorepos, `--add-dir` is one of the biggest quality-of-life flags. It is safer and more precise than broad full access.
Use `codex exec --json` for tooling, but pair it with `-o` or `--output-schema` so humans and machines both get a clean artifact.
When remote auth is flaky, device auth is usually faster than debugging browser callbacks.
Default web search is cached for a reason. Prefer cached unless you specifically need the latest docs or releases.
For parallel work, separate branches/worktrees beat one overloaded thread every time.
If Codex keeps making the same repo-specific mistake, that is a documentation/config problem, not a prompt problem. Fix it in AGENTS.md.
